From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Former Sydney passenger rail network operator

This article is about a former rail operator in Australia. For a former rail operator in New Zealand, see [Cityrail](/wiki/Cityrail "Cityrail").

CityRail

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| The *L7* logo of CityRail | |
| Agency overview | |
| Formed | 16 January 1989 |
| Dissolved | 30 June 2013 |
| Superseding agencies | * [Sydney Trains](/wiki/Sydney_Trains "Sydney Trains") * [NSW TrainLink](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink "NSW TrainLink") |
| Jurisdiction | [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") |
| Headquarters | [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") |
| Parent agency | [State Rail Authority](/wiki/State_Rail_Authority "State Rail Authority") (1989–2003) [RailCorp](/wiki/RailCorp "RailCorp") (2004–2013) |
| Key document | * *Transport Administration Act, 1988* (NSW) |
| Website | [www.cityrail.info](https://web.archive.org/web/20130601172447/http://cityrail.info/) |

**CityRail** was a passenger railway brand operated by [the State Rail Authority](/wiki/State_Rail_Authority "State Rail Authority") from 1989 to 2003 and by [RailCorp](/wiki/RailCorp "RailCorp") from 2004 to 2013 with services in and around [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales") and [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong"), the three largest cities in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia. It was established in January 1989 and abolished in June 2013 when it was superseded by [Sydney Trains](/wiki/Sydney_Trains "Sydney Trains") and [NSW TrainLink](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink "NSW TrainLink").

In June 2013, it operated [307 stations](/wiki/List_of_Sydney_railway_stations "List of Sydney railway stations") and over 2,060 kilometres of track, extending north to the upper [Hunter Valley](/wiki/Hunter_Valley "Hunter Valley"), south to the [Shoalhaven](/wiki/City_of_Shoalhaven "City of Shoalhaven") and [Southern Highlands](/wiki/Southern_Highlands_(New_South_Wales) "Southern Highlands (New South Wales)") regions and west to [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales"). In the year ended 30 June 2012, 306 million journeys were made on the network.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/7/7e/Cityrail_logo_2010-2013.gif)](/wiki/File:Cityrail_logo_2010-2013.gif)

The final logo of CityRail used until 2013

CityRail was established pursuant to the *[Transport Administration Act, 1988](https://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/nsw/consol_act//)* (NSW), and was first mentioned as an entity distinct from the [State Rail Authority](/wiki/State_Rail_Authority "State Rail Authority") in the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales") by then governor [James Rowland](/wiki/James_Rowland_(RAAF_officer) "James Rowland (RAAF officer)") on 21 February 1990.[[2]](#cite_note-2) CityRail adopted a blue and yellow version of the State Rail Authority *L7* logo, to fit into its new blue and yellow colour scheme.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[*[user-generated source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources#User-generated_content "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources")*] This logo continued to be in use until 2010, when it was replaced by the [Waratah](/wiki/Waratah "Waratah") logo of the [NSW Government](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

On 1 January 2004, [RailCorp](/wiki/RailCorp "RailCorp") assumed all functions of the State Rail Authority, and later the functions of the [Rail Infrastructure Corporation](/wiki/Rail_Infrastructure_Corporation "Rail Infrastructure Corporation") and Rail Access Corporation.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) This resulted in CityRail becoming a subsidiary of RailCorp, along with [CountryLink](/wiki/CountryLink "CountryLink").[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) CityRail became defunct on 1 July 2013, with [Sydney Trains](/wiki/Sydney_Trains "Sydney Trains") taking over suburban services and [NSW TrainLink](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink "NSW TrainLink") taking over Intercity and regional services.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Operational history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Operational history")]

When the CityRail brand was introduced the State Rail Authority was part way through taking delivery of 450 [Tangara](/wiki/Sydney_Trains_T_set "Sydney Trains T set") carriages. With these new carriages in service, the last single deck suburban sets were withdrawn in 1992, and the last [U set](/wiki/New_South_Wales_U_set "New South Wales U set") [interurban](/wiki/Interurban "Interurban") sets were withdrawn in 1996.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In February 1994, the first of 15 two-carriage [Endeavour railcars](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Endeavour_railcar "New South Wales Endeavour railcar") was delivered.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) These replaced [Class 620/720](/wiki/New_South_Wales_620/720_class_railcar "New South Wales 620/720 class railcar") railcars, [Class 900](/wiki/New_South_Wales_900/800_class_railcar "New South Wales 900/800 class railcar") railcars and locomotive-hauled stock.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In May 2000, the [Airport railway line](/wiki/Airport_railway_line,_Sydney "Airport railway line, Sydney") opened to CityRail services.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In July 2002, the first of 141 [M set](/wiki/Sydney_Trains_M_set "Sydney Trains M set") carriages entered service.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In November 2006, the first of seven two-carriage [Hunter railcar](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Hunter_railcar "New South Wales Hunter railcar") trains entered service.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

In December 2006, the first of 221 [Oscar](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink_H_set "NSW TrainLink H set") carriages entered service on the [South Coast Line](/wiki/South_Coast_Line "South Coast Line").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In February 2009, the [Epping to Chatswood railway line](/wiki/Epping_to_Chatswood_railway_line "Epping to Chatswood railway line") opened with shuttle services[[13]](#cite_note-13) and was integrated into the Northern Line service later that year.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In July 2011, the first [Waratah](/wiki/Sydney_Trains_A_set "Sydney Trains A set") trains entered service to replace the [S sets](/wiki/Sydney_Trains_S_set "Sydney Trains S set"), although it was announced in 2013 that some S sets would stay.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In October 2012, a new service from [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales") to Sydney commenced.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Operations")]

### Fleet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fleet")]

At the time of its cessation in June 2013, CityRail operated eight [electric multiple unit](/wiki/Electric_multiple_unit "Electric multiple unit") classes for suburban and interurban working and two [diesel multiple unit](/wiki/Diesel_multiple_unit "Diesel multiple unit") classes. All CityRail electric trains used [1500 V DC](/wiki/List_of_current_systems_for_electric_rail_traction "List of current systems for electric rail traction") [overhead electrification](/wiki/Overhead_electrification "Overhead electrification") and travel on 1,435mm [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") tracks. Double deck rollingstock was first introduced in 1964 and after 1996, all electric multiple units were double deck.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The CityRail network was divided into three sectors, based around three maintenance depots.[[17]](#cite_note-17) EMU trainsets were identified by target plates, which are exhibited on the front lower nearside of driving carriages.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Target designations and set numbers were used in identifying EMU trainsets. The composition and formations of trainsets, and the target designations were subject to alteration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

All [V sets](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink_V_set "NSW TrainLink V set") which operated on the [Newcastle](/wiki/Central_Coast_%26_Newcastle_Line "Central Coast & Newcastle Line") and [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_Line "Blue Mountains Line") lines, were serviced at Flemington Depot. All [M](/wiki/Sydney_Trains_M_set "Sydney Trains M set") and [H](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink_H_set "NSW TrainLink H set") sets, which had a green target plate, were serviced at [Eveleigh Maintenance Centre](/wiki/Eveleigh_Railway_Workshops "Eveleigh Railway Workshops").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Ticketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ticketing")]

For most of the brand's life CityRail's ticketing system was the [Automated Fare Collection System](/wiki/Automated_fare_collection_system_(Sydney) "Automated fare collection system (Sydney)") (AFC). Dating from 1992, it was based on [magnetic stripe](/wiki/Magnetic_stripe "Magnetic stripe") technology and was interoperable with the [Sydney Buses](/wiki/State_Transit_Authority "State Transit Authority") and [Sydney Ferries](/wiki/Sydney_Ferries "Sydney Ferries") systems. In later years the network was incorporated into the [MyZone](/wiki/MyZone "MyZone") ticketing system, which retained the AFC technology but extended the validity of multi-modal tickets to private buses and light rail.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Unlike the ticketing systems of other cities in Australia, most of CityRail's ticket prices were calculated on the distance travelled, and were proven to be the most expensive tickets of any major city public transport system.[[19]](#cite_note-Sydney_Morning_Herald-19)

### Performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Performance")]

According to the 2003 *Parry Report*, "The interaction of metropolitan, suburban, intercity and freight lines and services has resulted in an overly complex system.[[20]](#cite_note-Parry-20) This complexity has contributed in part to the organisation being widely criticised for poor reliability and safety. CityRail was also enormously expensive".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On-time running improved after new timetables were introduced in 2005 and 2006.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The newly introduced timetable increased station dwell and journey times.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In October 2012, a report published by [PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") found CityRail performed poorly compared to many metro services from 27 other major world cities. Sydney was ranked as the fourth-worst public train system while being proven to have the most expensive tickets of any major city public transport system.[[19]](#cite_note-Sydney_Morning_Herald-19)

## Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Network")]

### NightRide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: NightRide")]

Main article: [Nightride (bus service)](/wiki/Nightride_(bus_service) "Nightride (bus service)")

To provide a passenger service between midnight and 05:00 while leaving the tracks clear of trains for maintenance work, parallel bus services were established in 1989. [NightRide](/wiki/Nightride_(bus_service) "Nightride (bus service)") services operated typically at hourly intervals (some routes depart more frequently on weekends). NightRide services were run by private bus operators, and identified by route numbers beginning with "N". All valid CityRail tickets for a destination (apart from single tickets) were accepted on NightRide services.[[28]](#cite_note-35)

### Network overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Network overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Inside_central_railway_station._sydney.jpg/250px-Inside_central_railway_station._sydney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inside_central_railway_station._sydney.jpg)

The concourse of [Central Railway Station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney"), the main station on the CityRail network that opened in its present location in 1906

Most Intercity trains terminated at [Central](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney") while most suburban (except Carlingford Line) services proceeded through the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

CityRail also operated several Intercity services that terminate at Central station (though some services operated in the metro-style portions of the system in the peak hours). These lines stretched over 200 kilometres from Sydney, as far north as [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), as far west as [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales"), as far south-west as [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn,_New_South_Wales "Goulburn, New South Wales") and as far south as [Kiama](/wiki/Kiama,_New_South_Wales "Kiama, New South Wales") and [Port Kembla](/wiki/Port_Kembla,_New_South_Wales "Port Kembla, New South Wales"). Southern Highlands trains required a connection at Campbelltown as they ran into the city during peak hours only.

Regional services operated from the terminus station at [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_railway_station,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle railway station, New South Wales"), with local electric services to the [Central Coast](/wiki/Central_Coast,_New_South_Wales "Central Coast, New South Wales") and diesel services to [Telarah](/wiki/Telarah_railway_station "Telarah railway station") with some extending to [Dungog](/wiki/Dungog_railway_station "Dungog railway station") and [Scone](/wiki/Scone_railway_station "Scone railway station"). Diesel services also operated on the South Coast Line between [Kiama](/wiki/Kiama_railway_station "Kiama railway station") and [Bomaderry](/wiki/Bomaderry_railway_station "Bomaderry railway station").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The hub of the CityRail system was [Central station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney"), where most lines started and ended. Trains coming from the Airport & East Hills Line and Bankstown Line, after travelling anti-clockwise on the [City Circle](/wiki/City_Circle "City Circle"), sometimes terminated upon arrival at Central and proceeded to the [Macdonaldtown turnback](/wiki/Macdonaldtown_Carriage_Sheds "Macdonaldtown Carriage Sheds"). However, most trains continued on and become outward bound Inner West Line and South Line services. The reverse applied for trains coming from the Inner West and South lines, which, if not terminating, became outward bound trains on the Airport & East Hills and Bankstown lines respectively. In the same manner, all trains from the Western Line and Northern Line became North Shore Line trains once they reached Central.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As well as the Intercity services mentioned above, local services also ran in the [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales") local area during off-peak times, as part of the Newcastle & Central Coast Line. Local services also ran on the South Coast Line in the [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong") local area, usually between [Thirroul](/wiki/Thirroul_railway_station "Thirroul railway station") and [Port Kembla](/wiki/Port_Kembla_railway_station "Port Kembla railway station").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Passenger information systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Passenger information systems")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/CityRail_indicator_board.JPG/250px-CityRail_indicator_board.JPG)](/wiki/File:CityRail_indicator_board.JPG)

Dual horizontal, ceiling mounted screens as installed at many CityRail stations

Many CityRail stations were equipped with electronic passenger destination indicator boards. These provided information on the current time, next three available services, time due to arrival, destination route and the number of train carriages available.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[CityRail Chime](/wiki/File:CityRail_Chime.ogg "File:CityRail Chime.ogg")

The distinctive chime that is played prior to announcements

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

Due to the many differing types of stations that CityRail serves, their screens varied in form. In station where trains arrived at a higher frequency, 2 or more vertical LED screens were used on each platform to display the destination and arrival time whereas in low frequency areas 1 or 2 dual horizontal LED screens with a larger font were used. Manual destination indicator boards were still used at some lower patronage stations. In regional areas, a station relied on digital voice announcement for information on services. CBSM (Custom Built Sheet Metal) was responsible for the manufacture of many indicator board encasings.[[29]](#cite_note-36)

## Challenges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Challenges")]

The quality of the rail system was a matter of considerable political sensitivity. The performance of the [State Rail Authority](/wiki/State_Rail_Authority "State Rail Authority") and [RailCorp](/wiki/RailCorp "RailCorp") were questioned in regards to safety, training, a politically motivated focus on punctuality, management and workplace culture, with strong criticism from Justice [Peter McInerny](/wiki/Peter_McInerney "Peter McInerney") in his inquiries into the accidents at [Glenbrook](/wiki/Glenbrook_train_disaster "Glenbrook train disaster") and [Waterfall](/wiki/Waterfall_train_disaster "Waterfall train disaster").[[30]](#cite_note-37)[[31]](#cite_note-38) Transport is the third largest area of public expenditure in NSW, after health and education. A newspaper distributed to commuters, *[mX](/wiki/MX_(newspaper) "MX (newspaper)")*, and *[The Sydney Morning Herald](/wiki/The_Sydney_Morning_Herald "The Sydney Morning Herald")'s* "campaign for Sydney" kept transport at the top of the agenda ahead of the [2007 state election](/wiki/2007_New_South_Wales_state_election "2007 New South Wales state election"). In his 2003 interim report to the NSW Government, [Tom Parry](/wiki/Tom_Parry_(economist) "Tom Parry (economist)") was highly critical of CityRail. "It is hard to believe that taxpayers or the state are getting the best possible value from the large amounts of money being spent each year," he wrote.[[32]](#cite_note-39)

### Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/CityRail_yellow_line.jpg/250px-CityRail_yellow_line.jpg)](/wiki/File:CityRail_yellow_line.jpg)

A typical message asking passengers to stand behind the yellow line, and [tactile paving](/wiki/Tactile_paving "Tactile paving") near the edge of the platform

The safety of the CityRail network was called into question by two fatal accidents. The second [Glenbrook train disaster](/wiki/Glenbrook_train_disaster "Glenbrook train disaster") in 1999 killed seven people. In 2003, the [Waterfall train disaster](/wiki/Waterfall_train_disaster "Waterfall train disaster") killed seven.[[33]](#cite_note-40) Inquiries were conducted into both accidents. Official findings into the latter accident also blamed an "underdeveloped safety culture." There has been criticism of the way CityRail managed safety issues that arose, resulting in what the NSW Ministry of Transport called "a reactive approach to risk management".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

CityRail has launched public information campaigns regarding railway trespassing, prams and strollers, and falling between the platform and the train.[[34]](#cite_note-training-rules-41)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Crime")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Help_point_cityrail.jpg/250px-Help_point_cityrail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Help_point_cityrail.jpg)

An emergency help point

Crime committed on railway property has decreased by 32.9% since 2002, which RailCorp attributes to the deployment of some 600 [Transit Officers](/wiki/RailCorp_Transit_Officer "RailCorp Transit Officer") across the network.[[35]](#cite_note-RailCorp_Annual_Report_2007-42) All stations, including those that are remote or unstaffed, have emergency "help points" to put passengers in immediate contact with authorities should an incident occur. All stations are covered by [closed-circuit television](/wiki/Closed-circuit_television "Closed-circuit television") surveillance. However, a large amount of graffiti is still evident on some trains and the depots.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In recent years, concerns over terrorism have played a role in the management of the network. CityRail and other public transport providers participate in an ongoing public terrorism awareness campaign, *If you see something, say something*, adapted from a similar campaign in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[36]](#cite_note-43)

### Overloading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Overloading")]

In 2008, overloading of trains was found by the [Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal](/wiki/Independent_Pricing_and_Regulatory_Tribunal_of_New_South_Wales "Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal of New South Wales") (IPART)[[37]](#cite_note-44) to be a significant cause of delays. A survey undertaken by RailCorp in September 2011 revealed that 6 of the 13 lines had a maximum load that exceeded 135% (of the seated capacity) during the peak morning commute.[[38]](#cite_note-45)

### Public perception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CityRail&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Public perception")]

One result of CityRail's increasing problems was a sharp rise in public complaints and attacks against staff,[[39]](#cite_note-46) with a [Boston Consulting Group](/wiki/Boston_Consulting_Group "Boston Consulting Group") report claiming staff were actively hiding from irate customers wishing to complain about the service. The highly negative public perception of transit officers acting as ticket inspection officers and charging significant on-the-spot fines has also led to the organisation introducing anti-spitting fines and signage requesting commuters not abuse staff.[[40]](#cite_note-47)
